stories.headline: Denver Public Schools board votes unanimously to ban cellphones during school hours
stories.summary:
The Denver Public Schools Board of Education voted unanimously Monday evening to prohibit students from using cellphones and other personal communication devices, including smartwatches, during school hours beginning with the 2026-27 academic year in August. The policy applies to both district-run and charter schools and covers time between classes as well, though students who leave campus during lunch are exempt.

DPS, the state's largest school district, joins a growing number of Colorado and national school systems moving to restrict smartphone use during the school day. Previously, individual schools set their own phone policies; the new districtwide rule is more stringent than a new state law that merely requires each district to craft some form of cellphone policy by July 1.

More than 80 percent of community members surveyed by DPS supported banning phones during class, and 64 percent supported an all-day ban in middle and high schools. School leaders will determine how to enforce the policy, with options including locked pouches or classroom collection buckets.

"We know constant access to technology can be a distraction to learning," said board Treasurer Donald "DJ" Torres.

stories.headline: City Park bandshell deemed unsalvageable after March arson fire; demolition and replacement planned
stories.summary:
A structural assessment has determined that Denver's historic City Park bandshell, damaged in a suspected arson fire in March, cannot be saved and must be demolished, Mayor Mike Johnston and Denver Parks & Recreation announced Monday. The 97-year-old wooden structure, which hosts the annual City Park Jazz summer concert series, poses safety hazards in its remaining state.

Denver Parks & Recreation has contracted local firm Mundus Bishop to design a replacement bandshell. The plan calls for demolishing the existing structure this fall and beginning construction on a new one by year's end, with the goal of having it ready for City Park Jazz's 2027 season. The community has already donated more than $100,000 toward the project since April.

City Park Jazz, now in its 40th season, is currently using a rented mobile stage to continue its free Sunday afternoon concerts, which began June 7. A community meeting is scheduled for June 28 at City Park Jazz for residents to meet the design team and offer input on the new structure.

"We're committed to rebuilding a bandshell that reflects the cultural and recreational importance of City Park," said Jolon Clark, executive director of Denver Parks & Recreation.

stories.headline: Affidavit: 18-year-old showed no sign of braking before fatal DUI crash into Northglenn home
stories.summary:
An arrest affidavit reveals that 18-year-old Angelo Arias showed no sign of braking before his vehicle left the road and slammed into a Northglenn home on May 28, killing his 18-year-old passenger Paige Mahone. Northglenn police officers had attempted to stop Arias after observing him swerving between lanes on Washington Street around 11:50 p.m., but he sped away at approximately 60 mph in a 25 mph zone before crashing.

Arias told investigators he had taken several shots of liquor and smoked marijuana before the crash and had been driving for roughly an hour. Officers detected a strong odor of alcohol on his breath, and a search of his vehicle found marijuana paraphernalia and an empty shooter bottle. He initially could not recall whether anyone else was in the car with him.

Mahone was found breathing but unconscious on the passenger floor and was transported to a hospital, where she later died. Arias faces two counts of vehicular homicide — one for DUI and one for reckless driving — and is scheduled for a preliminary hearing in Adams County court on June 23.

stories.headline: Update: Identities released in Bent County prison deaths as CDOC remains tight-lipped on circumstances
stories.summary:
The Bent County coroner has identified the two inmates who died at the Bent County Correctional Facility on Saturday as 27-year-old Charles Gates and 59-year-old Michael Fisher. A third prisoner was injured and hospitalized. The Colorado Department of Corrections has offered no details about the nature of the incident and did not respond to follow-up questions Monday.

Fisher was serving life without parole for a first-degree murder conviction stemming from a 1996 Adams County killing. Gates was serving time across five separate cases, the longest a nine-year sentence for a 2023 motor vehicle theft in Douglas County; his record also included drug possession, assault, burglary, and other offenses.

Bent County Sheriff Jake Six said his office was called to the private CoreCivic-operated prison just after 11:22 p.m. Saturday for a report of a "missed count," meaning prisoners were not where they were supposed to be. Deputies secured the facility's perimeter before the missing inmates were located; Six declined to describe how they were found.

The Colorado Department of Corrections lifted the statewide prison visitation suspension it imposed following the deaths at all facilities except the Bent County Correctional Facility, which remains closed to visitors as the investigation continues.

stories.headline: Denver considers $3.5 million in incentives to launch three new international flights from DIA
stories.summary:
Denver city officials are weighing $3.5 million in incentives to attract three new international routes to Denver International Airport, according to the Denver Business Journal. United Airlines is among the carriers involved, with proposed service to destinations including Rome, Regina, and Punta Cana.

Under the proposed arrangement, United would receive credits against landing fees along with marketing reimbursements, contingent on maintaining service requirements over a two-year period. The incentive structure is designed to reduce the financial risk carriers face when launching new long-haul or international routes.

Expanding DIA's international reach has been a priority for Denver economic development officials, as direct international connections are seen as a key factor in attracting corporate headquarters and boosting tourism. The proposal would need formal approval before any agreements are finalized.

stories.headline: Cigna to exit Colorado's individual health insurance market in 2027, affecting more than 40,000 residents
stories.summary:
Cigna has officially notified the Colorado Division of Insurance that it will no longer offer plans on the state's individual health insurance market beginning in 2027, a move that will force more than 40,800 Coloradans to find new coverage. The company's plans, which are sold in 12 Front Range counties and Routt County, remain in effect through the end of 2026.

Cigna cited limited scale and the "highly dynamic" nature of the individual market in its withdrawal letter to Colorado Insurance Commissioner Michael Conway. The company announced the broader national exit during an investor call in late April. As recently as 2024, Cigna was the third-largest carrier in Colorado's individual market, behind Elevance Health and Kaiser Permanente.

Colorado Insurance Commissioner Michael Conway attributed the decision in part to the expiration of enhanced federal subsidies enacted during the COVID-19 pandemic. "It seems pretty evident that the reverberations from the tax credits expiring made it too difficult of a market for them to continue in," Conway said. The change affects only individually purchased plans; Cigna customers with employer-sponsored or Medicare Advantage coverage will not be impacted.

More than 300,000 Coloradans purchase health coverage through the individual market. Affected policyholders will be directed to seek new plans through Connect for Health Colorado during open enrollment later this year.

stories.headline: Polis signs law allowing Colorado to push back against federal mandates keeping coal plants open
stories.summary:
Governor Jared Polis signed legislation on June 4 allowing Colorado to resist federal directives that would force aging coal-fired power plants to remain operational beyond their planned retirement dates. The bill, HB26-1226, was prompted by Trump administration emergency orders aimed at keeping certain coal plants running, which Colorado lawmakers and utility operators say would increase costs for ratepayers and undermine the state's clean energy transition.

Under the new law, if coal plants are required to stay online past 2034, the Public Utilities Commission must receive detailed cost information from utilities and will have new financing tools to manage the expense of extended operations. The law also requires that any coal plants still operating use modern pollution controls and that the PUC approve replacement resources to keep Colorado on track toward its carbon reduction targets.

"Colorado is a leader in climate policy, and we should be able to continue planning our own energy future," said Senate Assistant Majority Leader Lisa Cutter, D-Jefferson County. Supporters noted that the Craig Unit 1 power plant — the specific facility at issue — is considered ready for retirement by both its operators and environmental groups.

Critics of the federal mandate argue it overrides years of state and utility planning while imposing additional costs on electricity customers across Colorado.

stories.headline: 'No Kings' movement plans Colorado watch parties June 14 for counter-programming to White House UFC event
stories.summary:
The "No Kings" protest movement will organize at least seven watch parties across Colorado on June 14 to livestream a New York City concert billed as counter-programming to a UFC event at the White House on the same day — President Donald Trump's 80th birthday. The "Rise Up, Speak Out" concert will feature celebrities including Jane Fonda and Bette Midler and will be headlined by Rufus Wainwright.

Watch parties are currently planned in Loveland, Granby, Golden, Alamosa, Longmont, Superior, and Crestone. More than 200 watch parties are scheduled nationally, according to organizers, who describe the event as a celebration of First Amendment rights including free speech, religion, press, assembly, and protest.

The "No Kings" movement has previously drawn tens of thousands of participants to protests in Denver and elsewhere in Colorado. The June 14 event is organized by the Committee for the First Amendment and will run concurrently with the UFC event at the White House.

stories.headline: Colorado PERA investment staff collected millions in bonuses even as retiree pension values declined
stories.summary:
An analysis by the Colorado Sun found that Colorado Public Employees' Retirement Association investment staff received some of their largest bonuses on record in the aftermath of 2022, the fund's worst investment year since the Great Recession, when PERA lost $9.8 billion. On average, investment staff more than doubled their take-home pay that year, collecting average incentive payments of $299,000 — representing a 124 percent premium above base salary — and nine officials received more than $400,000 each.

PERA officials defend the compensation structure, arguing it is necessary to attract and retain financial talent that would otherwise move to private-sector firms. Executive Director Andrew Roth told the Colorado Sun that outsourcing PERA's investment management to private firms would cost approximately $80 million more per year, and that over the past decade PERA's 8.3 percent investment return has ranked in the top 10 percent among public pension funds nationally.

Critics argue the pay structure is difficult to reconcile with the financial pressures placed on retirees it serves. Since 2010, PERA has implemented two rounds of benefit cuts and contribution increases to address a $29 billion funding gap, and retired state workers, teachers, and bus drivers have seen their pension values erode by roughly 21 percent due to inflation over that period.

Under Colorado law, incentive pay counts toward employees' own future retirement benefits, meaning some PERA staff will receive elevated pension payments for life as a result of the bonuses.

stories.headline: Red flag warnings issued for Denver metro Tuesday as dry, windy conditions raise wildfire risk
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service has issued red flag warnings for the Denver metro area and much of Colorado, set to take effect Tuesday afternoon and evening as wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph combine with dry conditions to create elevated wildfire danger. Forecasters warn that any fire ignited under these conditions could spread rapidly.

The warnings follow a weekend of near-record heat, with temperatures reaching the mid-90s across the metro area. The dry and windy pattern arrives as Colorado remains under a statewide drought emergency declared by Governor Polis, adding to concerns about fire risk across the Front Range and eastern plains.

CDOT has separately urged motorists to inspect their vehicles, noting that mechanical sparks from cars and trucks are a known cause of wildfires during dry, windy conditions. Residents are advised to avoid outdoor burning and to be aware of local fire restrictions.

stories.headline: CSU experts urge Colorado homeowners to 'embrace the beige' and rethink landscaping during historic drought
stories.summary:
Colorado State University landscape design and water experts are advising homeowners to adjust their expectations for lawns and gardens this summer as the state contends with a historic drought emergency. Assistant professor Scott Curry and CSU water specialist Lori Catalano coined the phrase "embrace the beige" to encourage residents to accept less-than-green turf and begin planning longer-term landscape changes that reduce water dependency.

Experts recommend homeowners upgrade to smart electronic irrigation controllers, which can cost around $150 and automatically adjust watering schedules based on real-time weather and evapotranspiration data. Curry also suggests professional irrigation audits to identify inefficiencies such as sprinklers watering pavement or running longer than necessary.

For those willing to make more permanent changes, experts recommend replacing traditional grass with drought-tolerant native plants and hardscape features. Some Colorado communities are already banning hand-watering of lawns as part of progressive drought restrictions, and CSU officials warn that garden hoses used without timers are among the most water-inefficient methods available to homeowners.

The advice comes as Colorado remains in Phase 3 drought conditions, with water managers and state officials urging broad conservation efforts across residential, agricultural, and municipal users.

stories.headline: Nuggets front office faces offseason challenge of finding value on minimum contracts
stories.summary:
With the NBA Finals underway between the New York Knicks and San Antonio Spurs, Denver Gazette beat writer Vinny Benedetto examines the lessons the Nuggets' front office can draw from players like Landry Shamet, who has been a pivotal contributor for New York on a minimum contract. Shamet went 11 for 12 from three-point range against Cleveland in the conference finals and has continued that production into the Finals.

The Nuggets face a similar roster-building challenge heading into the offseason: with Nikola Jokic's max contract consuming a large portion of the salary cap, Denver must find high-value players willing to sign for minimum deals. Last season, Tim Hardaway Jr. played on a veteran minimum worth $3.6 million, finished third in Sixth Man of the Year voting, and set a franchise record for three-pointers in a season; Bruce Brown also returned to Denver on a minimum.

However, the Nuggets cannot offer Hardaway or Brown more than 120 percent of their previous salaries to return, since the team does not hold their Bird rights — the same limitation Denver faced with Brown after the 2023 championship season. General manager Ben Tenzer and the front office must identify new minimum-contract targets for the coming year.

Among the names mentioned as potential fits are perimeter defenders Keon Ellis, Josh Okogie, Matisse Thybulle, and Gary Payton II, as well as frontcourt options including Marvin Bagley III and Andre Drummond.

weather.notable: Nearly 8 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: I like making people laugh. It's less complicated than making them think.

topStory.headline: U.S. Army Apache Helicopter Crashes Near Strait of Hormuz; Both Crew Members Rescued
topStory.summary:
A U.S. Army AH-64 Apache attack helicopter went down early Tuesday off the coast of Oman near the Strait of Hormuz while on patrol, the U.S. military's Central Command confirmed. The crash occurred at approximately 3:30 a.m. local time, and both crew members were rescued from the water within roughly two hours and are listed in stable condition.

President Donald Trump addressed the incident late Monday night while speaking to reporters at John F. Kennedy International Airport in New York, where he had been attending the NBA Finals. 'The pilots are fine, nobody injured,' Trump told reporters, adding that a formal report would be issued the following day. U.S. Central Command spokesman Capt. Timothy Hawkins confirmed that a U.S. Navy surface drone located and rescued the crew from the water.

The cause of the crash remains under investigation. The incident marks the first loss of an Apache helicopter since the United States began striking Iran on Feb. 28, a conflict that has also drawn in Israel and destabilized the broader Middle East region. The loss comes one day after Iran and Israel exchanged direct strikes in what officials described as the most serious breach yet of an April ceasefire.

Apache helicopters have played a central role in U.S. operations in the region, including enforcing a blockade on Iranian crude oil shipments and engaging Iranian small boats and drones. According to a Congressional Research Service report issued in May, the U.S. military has lost dozens of aircraft since the war began, including at least five fighter jets, seven Stratotanker refueling aircraft, a search-and-rescue helicopter, and more than two dozen drones.

The broader conflict has had significant global economic consequences, driving up energy prices and increasing the cost of basic goods including food. Diplomatic efforts to convert the April ceasefire into a permanent end to hostilities have so far failed, with Israel continuing to expand its military campaign in Lebanon against the Iranian-backed militia Hezbollah.
